beats.text: Earlier chapters used games to model the world; here the game becomes the training objective itself.

visual.sub: The loss surface is co-created by the learner

beats.text: Static supervised learning has a ceiling: once you match the fixed labels, the gradient goes quiet.
A game-based objective never goes quiet because the opponent keeps raising the bar as you improve.
Three families dominate practice: adversarial generation, self-play, and many agents sharing a world.
What unifies them is the minimax skeleton, a value one party maximizes and another minimizes.
Supervised learning imitates a teacher; a game manufactures one, supplying the next harder example.
A GAN pits a generator turning noise into fakes against a discriminator scoring how real a sample looks.
Goodfellow and colleagues wrote both networks into one two-player zero-sum value function.
Fixing the generator, calculus gives the optimal critic in closed form as a ratio of densities.
When the generator matches the data, the perfect critic is reduced to a coin flip of one half.
Substituting the best critic collapses the game onto a divergence with a floor of negative log four.

visual.label: Game value C(G) at the optimum

beats.text: The framing is clean but the optimization is hard, with instability and mode collapse in practice.
The cleanest game-as-curriculum is an agent playing against itself, with no human data and no fixed teacher.
AlphaZero uses one network outputting move probabilities and a scalar value between minus one and one.

visual.note: Policy and value from one state

beats.text: Training regresses the value toward outcomes and pulls the raw policy toward the search-improved one.
A minimax value backup flips the sign on the child value, since your gain is your copy's loss.
Against a fixed teacher the curve flattens at the teacher's strength; a frozen opponent is a ceiling.
In self-play the opponent rises with you, an open-ended ratchet driving AlphaStar, OpenAI Five, and Pluribus.
Multi-agent RL drops N learners into a shared world, each with its own policy and reward.

visual.sub: N learners in one moving world

beats.text: The hard part is non-stationarity: from one agent's view the others are an environment that keeps changing.
Centralized training with decentralized execution lets a critic see joint actions while actors stay local.
The Stag Hunt has two equilibria: hunting stag together pays four, hunting hare alone safely pays three.
Stag beats hare only when expected payoff four-q exceeds three, so your partner must cooperate often.
Below the 0.75 basin boundary populations collapse to safe hare; above it they climb to payoff-dominant stag.
Two final places the game frame is load-bearing: designing games, and defending against them.

visual.sub: Designing games, and defending against them

beats.text: Mechanism design runs the arrow backward, choosing rules so self-interested play yields the outcome you want.
RLHF is a mechanism; reward hacking is what happens when the incentive structure is mis-specified.
Adversarial training is a minimax where an inner maximizer perturbs the input inside an epsilon-ball.
The model manufactures its own worst case each step, a self-generating curriculum of hard examples.
The lesson stated once more: to make a system robust, train it against an adversary that improves alongside it.

visual.text: A learning opponent is a teacher without a ceiling
visual.sub: The minimax skeleton creates strategy, not just analyzes it
